We report the first-known low-temperature eclogite-facies meta-felsic gneisses from the Beishan Orogenic Collage in the Central Asian Orogenic Belt in NW China. Petrological examination, mineral compositions and phase equilibrium modeling reveal that these gneisses experienced three metamorphic stages, with peak-pressure assemblage of garnet, phengite, paragonite, epidote, hornblende, plagioclase, and rutile at eclogite-facies conditions of ∼14 kbar and ∼580−590 °C, representing an apparent geothermal gradient of ∼11−13 °C/km. A clockwise pressure−temperature path involving decompression heating to amphibolite-facies at ∼9 kbar and ∼630−650 °C during exhumation is indicative of a transition from subduction to accretion orogenesis. Zircon and garnet U-Pb geochronology indicate that the low-temperature eclogite-facies metamorphism occurred at ca. 279 Ma, followed by amphibolite-facies metamorphism at ca. 273 Ma. This study provides key evidence supporting subduction in the Beishan Orogenic Collage during the Permian, addressing the long-standing controversy over its tectonic environment during the late Paleozoic. We demonstrate that closure of the Paleo-Asian Ocean at the Beishan Orogenic Collage occurred after the Middle Permian.

item.title: Lithospheric Melting and Gold Mineralization in the Southwestern Cathaysia Block: Constraints From Magnetotelluric Evidence
item.dc:creator: Yufeng Chen
Jiayong Yan
Juzhi Deng
Hui Chen
Yuexin You
Hui Yu
Zhen Deng
Fan Luo
Xu Wang
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Mesozoic Paleo‐Pacific plate subduction was dominant and drove intense continental reworking in South China, coupled with voluminous magmatism and episodic mineralization events. However, the lack of detailed geophysical constraints on the lithospheric architecture of southwestern Cathaysia Block hinders resolution of its alteration mechanisms and metallogenic evolution. We acquired magnetotelluric array data at 82 sites across southwestern Cathaysia Block, constraining the lithospheric resistivity architecture of this region. Integrating laboratory measurements of mantle minerals, broadband seismic data, and geothermal constraints, we quantitatively constrain the origin of lithospheric mantle conductivity anomalies. Research findings indicate that the crust in the study area is predominantly characterized by a high‐resistivity background, interspersed with linear conductive anomalies spatially correlated with fault zones. The conductive features in the lithospheric mantle likely result from the combined effects of partial melting and water in the mantle. Based on the regional tectonic framework, we propose that water released from previously subducted slabs and associated metasomatism weakened the lithosphere, potentially leading to delamination of the lithospheric root. This process, accompanied by slab collapse, triggered asthenospheric upwelling and thermo‐mechanical perturbations. Thermal erosion by the upwelling asthenosphere facilitated decompression melting within the thinned lithospheric mantle and the overlying, extended crust. The Paleo‐Pacific subduction system provided the necessary conduits for sourcing and transporting Au‐rich fluids, ultimately facilitating gold mineralization in the study area. Published: 2026-06-11T11:15:47+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.621 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yufeng Chen; Jiayong Yan; Juzhi Deng; Hui Chen; Yuexin You; Hui Yu; Zhen Deng; Fan Luo; Xu Wang

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb032615

Relevance: 0.621 (must_read)

Abstract Mesozoic Paleo‐Pacific plate subduction was dominant and drove intense continental reworking in South China, coupled with voluminous magmatism and episodic mineralization events. However, the lack of detailed geophysical constraints on the lithospheric architecture of southwestern Cathaysia Block hinders resolution of its alteration mechanisms and metallogenic evolution. We acquired magnetotelluric array data at 82 sites across southwestern Cathaysia Block, constraining the lithospheric resistivity architecture of this region. Integrating laboratory measurements of mantle minerals, broadband seismic data, and geothermal constraints, we quantitatively constrain the origin of lithospheric mantle conductivity anomalies. Research findings indicate that the crust in the study area is predominantly characterized by a high‐resistivity background, interspersed with linear conductive anomalies spatially correlated with fault zones. The conductive features in the lithospheric mantle likely result from the combined effects of partial melting and water in the mantle. Based on the regional tectonic framework, we propose that water released from previously subducted slabs and associated metasomatism weakened the lithosphere, potentially leading to delamination of the lithospheric root. This process, accompanied by slab collapse, triggered asthenospheric upwelling and thermo‐mechanical perturbations. Thermal erosion by the upwelling asthenosphere facilitated decompression melting within the thinned lithospheric mantle and the overlying, extended crust. The Paleo‐Pacific subduction system provided the necessary conduits for sourcing and transporting Au‐rich fluids, ultimately facilitating gold mineralization in the study area.

item.title: In Situ U-Pb Dating of Hydrothermal Monazite From Metasediment-Hosted Orogenic Au Deposits: Constraints on the Age and Geotectonic Setting of Au Mineralization in the West Qinling Orogen, Central China
item.dc:creator: Chong-Guo He
Yu-Zhao Li
Daniel J. Kontak
Xiao-Ye Jin
Shi-Guang Du
Zheng Xu
Huan Tao
Hang-Jin Jiang
Jian-Wei Li
item.description: Abstract The general lack of absolute ages for most Au deposits in the well-endowed West Qinling orogen (central China) has hampered establishment of the exact temporal framework of regional Au mineralization during the geotectonic evolution of this orogen. This study presents new in situ U-Pb ages on hydrothermal monazite in native gold-bearing ores from three world-class Au deposits (Liba, Baguamiao, and Shuangwang) in the West Qinling orogen. The dating results indicate that these deposits formed synchronously at ca. 220 Ma. In all cases, these ages overlap with new U-Pb dates for felsic and mafic magmatism in the deposit settings. Our new age data, when combined with independent geologic evidence and previous geochronological studies, suggest orogen-wide Au mineralization was controlled by the Triassic orogenic processes involving westward diachronous subduction and closure of the Mianlue ocean and subsequent oblique continental convergence of the Yangtze craton and the South Qinling terrane. During ca. 250 to 225 Ma, Au mineralization in the western part of the West Qinling orogen is attributed to oceanic subduction, whereas in the eastern part it is related to incipient continental collision. At ca. 225 to 210 Ma, continental collision culminated in the final formation of the West Qinling orogen, during which slab tearing induced upwelling of the asthenosphere and melting of lithospheric mantle and continental crust, as is reflected by the widespread granitic magmatism and synchronous mafic dikes. These latter events facilitated extensive crustal fluid flow that generated the significant Au endowment in the West Qinling orogen as represented by the dated three deposits. Published: 2026-06-08T14:31:47+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.619 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chong-Guo He; Yu-Zhao Li; Daniel J. Kontak; Xiao-Ye Jin; Shi-Guang Du; Zheng Xu; Huan Tao; Hang-Jin Jiang; Jian-Wei Li

Journal: Economic Geology

DOI: 10.5382/econgeo.5242

Relevance: 0.619 (must_read)

Abstract The general lack of absolute ages for most Au deposits in the well-endowed West Qinling orogen (central China) has hampered establishment of the exact temporal framework of regional Au mineralization during the geotectonic evolution of this orogen. This study presents new in situ U-Pb ages on hydrothermal monazite in native gold-bearing ores from three world-class Au deposits (Liba, Baguamiao, and Shuangwang) in the West Qinling orogen. The dating results indicate that these deposits formed synchronously at ca. 220 Ma. In all cases, these ages overlap with new U-Pb dates for felsic and mafic magmatism in the deposit settings. Our new age data, when combined with independent geologic evidence and previous geochronological studies, suggest orogen-wide Au mineralization was controlled by the Triassic orogenic processes involving westward diachronous subduction and closure of the Mianlue ocean and subsequent oblique continental convergence of the Yangtze craton and the South Qinling terrane. During ca. 250 to 225 Ma, Au mineralization in the western part of the West Qinling orogen is attributed to oceanic subduction, whereas in the eastern part it is related to incipient continental collision. At ca. 225 to 210 Ma, continental collision culminated in the final formation of the West Qinling orogen, during which slab tearing induced upwelling of the asthenosphere and melting of lithospheric mantle and continental crust, as is reflected by the widespread granitic magmatism and synchronous mafic dikes. These latter events facilitated extensive crustal fluid flow that generated the significant Au endowment in the West Qinling orogen as represented by the dated three deposits.

item.title: Crustal melting and geodynamics in the Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Huixia Ding
Zeming Zhang
Richard M. Palin
item.description: Partial melting and ductile flow within thickened lower crust have long been considered key characteristics of the Tibetan Plateau; however, the spatiotemporal variations and geodynamic causes of crustal melting remain debated. Using a comprehensive dataset (>5300 samples) of Cenozoic metamorphic and magmatic rocks across the entire Tibetan Plateau orogen, we show that the early stage of India–Asia collision (~60–45 Ma) records crustal melting within normal-thickness and slightly thickened lower crust in the frontal region of the orogen, driven by asthenospheric upwelling associated with rollback and breakoff of Neo-Tethyan subducted oceanic lithosphere. By contrast, the late collisional stage (~45–8 Ma) records extensive and long-lived crustal melting within thickened lower crust in the orogenic front, and intermittent and localized crustal melting in the orogenic hinterland, driven by intracrustal radioactive heating and mantle heat flux driven by delamination of overthickened continental lithosphere. Since 8 Ma, the vast orogen has entered a waning phase of crustal melting due to continued underthrusting of old, thick, and cold Indian continental lithosphere, which cuts off mantle heat. Large collisional orogens are therefore major sites of continental crustal reworking rather than growth, but not all exhibit long-distance flow of partially molten deep crust through different tectonic units. Published: 2026-06-09T16:13:02+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.619 (must_read)

Authors: Huixia Ding; Zeming Zhang; Richard M. Palin

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105573

Relevance: 0.619 (must_read)

Partial melting and ductile flow within thickened lower crust have long been considered key characteristics of the Tibetan Plateau; however, the spatiotemporal variations and geodynamic causes of crustal melting remain debated. Using a comprehensive dataset (>5300 samples) of Cenozoic metamorphic and magmatic rocks across the entire Tibetan Plateau orogen, we show that the early stage of India–Asia collision (~60–45 Ma) records crustal melting within normal-thickness and slightly thickened lower crust in the frontal region of the orogen, driven by asthenospheric upwelling associated with rollback and breakoff of Neo-Tethyan subducted oceanic lithosphere. By contrast, the late collisional stage (~45–8 Ma) records extensive and long-lived crustal melting within thickened lower crust in the orogenic front, and intermittent and localized crustal melting in the orogenic hinterland, driven by intracrustal radioactive heating and mantle heat flux driven by delamination of overthickened continental lithosphere. Since 8 Ma, the vast orogen has entered a waning phase of crustal melting due to continued underthrusting of old, thick, and cold Indian continental lithosphere, which cuts off mantle heat. Large collisional orogens are therefore major sites of continental crustal reworking rather than growth, but not all exhibit long-distance flow of partially molten deep crust through different tectonic units.

item.title: Late oligocene Cu-Mo mineralization at West Zafarghand, Iran: Evidence for a short-lived magmatic-hydrothermal system
item.dc:creator: Mohammad Fallah
Hemayat Jamali
Ahmad Rabiee
Federico Rossetti
Zahra Alaminia
David Selby
Fabrizio Di Fiore
item.description: This study investigates the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution of the West Zafarghand porphyry CuMo deposit in the central Urumieh-Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), Iran using field and petrographic observations, integrated with fluid inclusion microthermometry, TitaniQ thermometry, UPb zircon and Re Os molybdenite geochronology. The deposit is hosted by late Eocene volcanic rocks, intruded by granodiorite and granite porphyries and andesitic dykes swarm. Mineralization includes magnetite-bornite-chalcopyrite and chalcopyrite ± pyrite assemblages, associated with potassic and chlorite-sericite alteration, followed by molybdenite mineralization during transition to phyllic and propylitic, carbonate-bearing alteration. UPb zircon geochronology constrains the volcanic host rocks and the granodiorite porphyry to ca. 34–33 Ma, whereas the granite porphyry and the dykes are late Oligocene in age (ca.24–23 Ma). Overlapping late Oligocene Re Os molybdenite ages indicate a tight genetic link between CuMo mineralization and the emplacement of the younger granitic magmatism. Whole-rock geochemistry indicates derivation from subduction-modified mantle meltsand lower-crust interaction, with the late Oligocene magmatism displaying a weak adakite-like affinity (Sr/Y up to ~50). Fluid inclusion data define fluid evolution at shallow crustal levels, recording early-stage boiling and phase separation, followed by cooling from ~570 °C in potassic stage at ~2.2 km depth to ~217 °C in propylitic alteration at ~1.5 km depth. The study highlights: (i) robust geochronology for constraining magmatism and ore formation; (ii) late-stage, highly fractionated, fertile intrusions with weak adakite-like affinity as responsible for mineralization; (iii) fluid boiling promoted Cu-o mineralization, and fluid neutralization favored molybdenite–carbonate precipitation. Published: 2026-06-11T17:56:54+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.617 (consider)

This study investigates the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution of the West Zafarghand porphyry CuMo deposit in the central Urumieh-Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), Iran using field and petrographic observations, integrated with fluid inclusion microthermometry, TitaniQ thermometry, UPb zircon and Re Os molybdenite geochronology. The deposit is hosted by late Eocene volcanic rocks, intruded by granodiorite and granite porphyries and andesitic dykes swarm. Mineralization includes magnetite-bornite-chalcopyrite and chalcopyrite ± pyrite assemblages, associated with potassic and chlorite-sericite alteration, followed by molybdenite mineralization during transition to phyllic and propylitic, carbonate-bearing alteration. UPb zircon geochronology constrains the volcanic host rocks and the granodiorite porphyry to ca. 34–33 Ma, whereas the granite porphyry and the dykes are late Oligocene in age (ca.24–23 Ma). Overlapping late Oligocene Re Os molybdenite ages indicate a tight genetic link between CuMo mineralization and the emplacement of the younger granitic magmatism. Whole-rock geochemistry indicates derivation from subduction-modified mantle meltsand lower-crust interaction, with the late Oligocene magmatism displaying a weak adakite-like affinity (Sr/Y up to ~50). Fluid inclusion data define fluid evolution at shallow crustal levels, recording early-stage boiling and phase separation, followed by cooling from ~570 °C in potassic stage at ~2.2 km depth to ~217 °C in propylitic alteration at ~1.5 km depth. The study highlights: (i) robust geochronology for constraining magmatism and ore formation; (ii) late-stage, highly fractionated, fertile intrusions with weak adakite-like affinity as responsible for mineralization; (iii) fluid boiling promoted Cu-o mineralization, and fluid neutralization favored molybdenite–carbonate precipitation.

item.title: Origin of negative Δ33S anomalies and Δ36S–Δ33S trends in 3700 Ma Isua sulfides: Searching for evidence of Eoarchean life in dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks
item.dc:creator: Janaína N. Ávila
Allen P. Nutman
Vickie C. Bennett
Clark R.L. Friend
Martin Van Kranendonk
item.description: Proof of life in the oldest rock record is complex and at the limit of current scientific capabilities. However, investigations using the covariation between minor isotopes of sulfur (Δ33S and Δ36S) reveals the potential of using the sulfur isotope system to discriminate between biologically-influenced and entirely non-biological signatures. This paper reports new in-situ four sulfur isotope data measured on rare pyrite grains in Isua supracrustal belt (Greenland) 3700 Ma metamorphosed dolomitic rocks with primary sedimentary structures. The sulfide grains have negative Δ33S and positive Δ36S values indicative of photolytic sulfate as their primary sulfur source. The limited spread in δ34S values relative to an inferred Archean seawater sulfate reservoir, along with covariation between the minor sulfur isotopes (Δ33S and Δ36S) can be modelled by microbial sulfate reduction under conditions of low sulfate concentration (<100 μM). Potential abiogenic origins for these signatures are also considered, but they either fail to reproduce the observed sulfur isotope relationships or require reaction conditions or sulfur sources that are difficult to reconcile with the geological context of the Isua meta-dolomitic rocks. Our findings reinforce earlier interpretations that these pyrite-bearing dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks have rare primary sedimentary structures preserved along with trace-element signatures indicative of biomediation in a marine-like environment. Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms. Published: 2026-06-12T00:10:36+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.615 (consider)

Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms.

item.title: Mesozoic continental crustal growth: A comprehensive review of the accretion-arc system in the Jilin-Heilongjiang high-pressure belt
item.dc:creator: Zhong-Hua Xin
Jian-Bo Zhou
Wen-Jiao Xiao
Jiang-Tao Han
Gong-Yu Li
Xiao-Miao Tan
Hong-Da Liang
Zhuo Chen
Hong-Yan Wang
Ning-Chen Sun
Rui Gao
item.description: The development of accretion-arc systems represents a fundamental mechanism of continental crustal growth and records key tectonic transitions during oceanic plate subduction. The Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt, eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, constitutes a classic accretion-arc system and provides an exceptional site for investigating geodynamic evolution. This review synthesizes geochronological, geochemical, deep seismic reflection, and magnetotelluric data, combined with other typical cases, to reveal the structural characteristics, formation mechanism, and continental growth geodynamic of the accretion-arc systems. Available geochronological constraints indicate that the protoliths of both the Zhangguangcai Complex and Heilongjiang Complex formed predominantly during the Permian and underwent successive metamorphism in the Early Jurassic, implying a unified tectonic regime linked to the evolution of the Heilongjiang Ocean. Arc-related magmatism displays typical subduction-related geochemical affinities, but is distinguished by relatively low 87Sr/86Sr ratios and high positive zircon εHf(t) values, reflecting substantial input from juvenile subduction-accretionary materials and marking the transition from accretionary growth to arc magmatism. Integrated seismic reflection and magnetotelluric imaging reveal imbricated, small-scale, short-arc reflectors with high resistivity beneath the accretionary complexes, underlain by mid-lower crustal high-conductivity, anti-U-shaped reflectors interpreted as deep-seated arc magmatic systems. These observations indicate that the Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt preserves a complete evolutionary sequence from accretionary wedge formation to arc magmatism. The fundamental geodynamic mechanism was dominated by prolonged low-angle westward subduction and subsequent plate rollback of the young Heilongjiang Ocean. Voluminous inputs of juvenile material underscore the critical role of accretion-arc systems in continental crustal growth. Published: 2026-06-13T00:54:36+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.605 (consider)

Available geochronological constraints indicate that the protoliths of both the Zhangguangcai Complex and Heilongjiang Complex formed predominantly during the Permian and underwent successive metamorphism in the Early Jurassic, implying a unified tectonic regime linked to the evolution of the Heilongjiang Ocean. The fundamental geodynamic mechanism was dominated by prolonged low-angle westward subduction and subsequent plate rollback of the young Heilongjiang Ocean. Voluminous inputs of juvenile material underscore the critical role of accretion-arc systems in continental crustal growth.

item.title: Timing of magmatic–hydrothermal activity and gold mineralization at the Jiudian Gold Deposit, Jiaodong Peninsula, Eastern China: Constraints from integrated U–Pb and Rb–Sr geochronology
item.dc:creator: Zuowen Dai
Yuling Xie
Zhendong Cai
Yunwei Qu
Xingyuan Wang
Leilei Dong
Dunfang Qiu
Jun Hu
Zichong Song
item.description: The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. Zircon U–Pb analyses of pegmatites yield weighted mean 206Pb/238U ages of 145.9 ± 1.6 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma, whereas garnet U–Pb dating produces indistinguishable lower-intercept ages of 145.8 ± 1.2 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma for Grt-1 and Grt-2, respectively. These results demonstrate that skarn formation was coeval with pegmatitic magmatism at ∼145 Ma. In contrast, hydrothermal muscovite and sericite intimately associated with electrum, the principal gold-bearing phase, yield Rb–Sr isochron ages of 119.8 ± 8.6 Ma and 119.2 ± 2.7 Ma, constraining the timing of gold mineralization to ∼120 Ma. Combined with previously published geochronological data, our results indicate that the ∼145 Ma skarn-forming event represents a gold-barren magmatic–hydrothermal episode genetically related to emplacement of the Linglong granite, whereas economically significant gold mineralization occurred ∼25 Myr later during an independent hydrothermal event. The ∼120 Ma mineralization age is synchronous with the widespread intrusion of mafic dikes in the Jiudian area and the broader Jiaodong gold province, implying that mantle-derived magmatism likely played a key role in triggering gold mineralization. This study provides robust evidence for the temporal decoupling between early granite-related hydrothermal activity and late gold mineralization in the Jiudian deposit, and further supports a multi-stage metallogenic model involving discrete magmatic-hydrothermal events for the Jiaodong gold province. Published: 2026-06-14T17:58:20+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.604 (consider)

The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. Zircon U–Pb analyses of pegmatites yield weighted mean 206Pb/238U ages of 145.9 ± 1.6 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma, whereas garnet U–Pb dating produces indistinguishable lower-intercept ages of 145.8 ± 1.2 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma for Grt-1 and Grt-2, respectively. These results demonstrate that skarn formation was coeval with pegmatitic magmatism at ∼145 Ma. In contrast, hydrothermal muscovite and sericite intimately associated with electrum, the principal gold-bearing phase, yield Rb–Sr isochron ages of 119.8 ± 8.6 Ma and 119.2 ± 2.7 Ma, constraining the timing of gold mineralization to ∼120 Ma. Combined with previously published geochronological data, our results indicate that the ∼145 Ma skarn-forming event represents a gold-barren magmatic–hydrothermal episode genetically related to emplacement of the Linglong granite, whereas economically significant gold mineralization occurred ∼25 Myr later during an independent hydrothermal event. The ∼120 Ma mineralization age is synchronous with the widespread intrusion of mafic dikes in the Jiudian area and the broader Jiaodong gold province, implying that mantle-derived magmatism likely played a key role in triggering gold mineralization. This study provides robust evidence for the temporal decoupling between early granite-related hydrothermal activity and late gold mineralization in the Jiudian deposit, and further supports a multi-stage metallogenic model involving discrete magmatic-hydrothermal events for the Jiaodong gold province.

item.title: Progressive crustal melting during low-angle subduction to slab rollback: insights from late Mesozoic granitoids in the Liaodong Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Jin-Feng Sun
Jin-Hui Yang
Ya-dong Wu
Yu-Sheng Zhu
Lei Xu
Bao-Quan Zhou
Ji-Heng Zhang
Yan-Bin Zhang
item.description: Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. Four chronological stages are identified for the Late Mesozoic magmatism: Early−Middle Jurassic granitoids (182–165 Ma), Late Jurassic granitoids (159–155 Ma), Early Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites (129–123 Ma), and Early Cretaceous I-type granites (123–118 Ma). Jurassic granitoids were generated mainly by relatively high-pressure, low-temperature (ca. 756 °C), water-fluxed melting of lower crustal materials with garnet as a residual phase. Higher δ18O and strongly negative εHf(t) values of Late Jurassic granites indicate relatively more felsic source rocks than those of Early−Middle Jurassic granites. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. This temporal progression can be interpreted as a response to the transition from continental thickening to thinning, related to the shift from low-angle subduction to slab rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, highlighting how the petrogenetic diversity of granitoids can be used to trace crustal evolution in response to subduction dynamics. Published: 2026-06-14T16:39:36+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.604 (consider)

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123565

Relevance: 0.604 (consider)

Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. Four chronological stages are identified for the Late Mesozoic magmatism: Early−Middle Jurassic granitoids (182–165 Ma), Late Jurassic granitoids (159–155 Ma), Early Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites (129–123 Ma), and Early Cretaceous I-type granites (123–118 Ma). Jurassic granitoids were generated mainly by relatively high-pressure, low-temperature (ca. 756 °C), water-fluxed melting of lower crustal materials with garnet as a residual phase. Higher δ18O and strongly negative εHf(t) values of Late Jurassic granites indicate relatively more felsic source rocks than those of Early−Middle Jurassic granites. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. This temporal progression can be interpreted as a response to the transition from continental thickening to thinning, related to the shift from low-angle subduction to slab rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, highlighting how the petrogenetic diversity of granitoids can be used to trace crustal evolution in response to subduction dynamics.

item.title: Magmatism and gold mineralization in the Tianmashan skarn deposit, Tongling district, China
item.dc:creator: Shiwei Wang
Tao Gao
Taofa Zhou
Yu Fan
Haojie Xie
Shuo Wu
Jianzhong Du
Jianping Wang
Ziteng Li
item.description: Skarn gold deposits constitute an important gold resource in China, and numerous porphyry-skarn Cu-Au and skarn Au systems distributed along the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt. However, the genesis of gold-dominant skarn systems in this region remains poorly constrained. The Tianmashan gold deposit, the largest skarn Au deposit in the Tongling district, provides an excellent opportunity to investigate the processes responsible for gold mineralization. This study integrates zircon and garnet U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon Hf isotopes, and sulfide sulfur isotope and trace-element data to constrain the timing, magma source, and ore-forming processes. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of magmatic zircon and hydrothermal garnet yields ages of 137.1 ± 1.0 Ma and 137.1 ± 2.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that magmatism and gold mineralization were coeval during the Early Cretaceous. The quartz diorite porphyry exhibits adakitic geochemical characteristics, indicating derivation from partial melting of a thickened lower crust. Low zircon εHf(t) values (−18.9 to −12.0) further suggest involvement of both juvenile and ancient crustal components, with the juvenile lower crust likely serving as an important metal reservoir for the ore-forming magmas. Zircon oxybarometry indicates elevated magmatic oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ + 0.9 to + 4.3), which was likely enhanced by the input of oxidized mantle-derived magma during crust–mantle magma mixing. Sulfide trace element and sulfur isotope data indicate that the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic-hydrothermal, with a limited contribution from sedimentary-derived fluids during the late-stage mineralization. Gold was primarily sourced from magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, whereas the formation of the interlayer replacement-type orebodies was likely facilitated by mixing between magmatic-hydrothermal fluids and sedimentary-derived fluids. Such fluid mixing may have lowered the oxidation state of the hydrothermal system and promoted gold precipitation. Published: 2026-06-10T23:59:25+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.601 (consider)

Authors: Shiwei Wang; Tao Gao; Taofa Zhou; Yu Fan; Haojie Xie; Shuo Wu; Jianzhong Du; Jianping Wang; Ziteng Li

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107374

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

Skarn gold deposits constitute an important gold resource in China, and numerous porphyry-skarn Cu-Au and skarn Au systems distributed along the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt. However, the genesis of gold-dominant skarn systems in this region remains poorly constrained. The Tianmashan gold deposit, the largest skarn Au deposit in the Tongling district, provides an excellent opportunity to investigate the processes responsible for gold mineralization. This study integrates zircon and garnet U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon Hf isotopes, and sulfide sulfur isotope and trace-element data to constrain the timing, magma source, and ore-forming processes. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of magmatic zircon and hydrothermal garnet yields ages of 137.1 ± 1.0 Ma and 137.1 ± 2.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that magmatism and gold mineralization were coeval during the Early Cretaceous. The quartz diorite porphyry exhibits adakitic geochemical characteristics, indicating derivation from partial melting of a thickened lower crust. Low zircon εHf(t) values (−18.9 to −12.0) further suggest involvement of both juvenile and ancient crustal components, with the juvenile lower crust likely serving as an important metal reservoir for the ore-forming magmas. Zircon oxybarometry indicates elevated magmatic oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ + 0.9 to + 4.3), which was likely enhanced by the input of oxidized mantle-derived magma during crust–mantle magma mixing. Sulfide trace element and sulfur isotope data indicate that the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic-hydrothermal, with a limited contribution from sedimentary-derived fluids during the late-stage mineralization. Gold was primarily sourced from magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, whereas the formation of the interlayer replacement-type orebodies was likely facilitated by mixing between magmatic-hydrothermal fluids and sedimentary-derived fluids. Such fluid mixing may have lowered the oxidation state of the hydrothermal system and promoted gold precipitation.

item.title: Chemical mohometry, thick and thin: Critical review and new steps forward
item.dc:creator: Matthew J. Kohn
Allen F. Glazner
Chris Yakymchuk
Armando Lopez
Gloria Roman
item.description: Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling. Published: 2026-06-14T16:32:25+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.599 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Matthew J. Kohn; Allen F. Glazner; Chris Yakymchuk; Armando Lopez; Gloria Roman

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105583

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling.

item.title: Neoarchean sanukitoids, metavolcanic rocks and TTGs of the northern Yangtze Craton, China: Insights into petrogenesis and crust-mantle interactions
item.dc:creator: Peiwen Liu
Xiaozhong Ding
Yanxue Liu
Tingdong Li
item.description: The northern part of the Yangtze Craton is one of the few regions in present-day South China with geological records dating back to the Archean. However, the geodynamic processes of Neoarchean continental crust formation and evolution in the northern Yangtze Craton remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. These features suggest that they formed via partial melting of a subducted oceanic slab. (4) The ~2.5 Ga Douling dioritic gneisses and quartz dioritic gneisses exhibit characteristics typical of Archean sanukitoids and formed by partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and melts derived from sediments and oceanic slabs. These findings, together with the presence of contemporaneous subduction-related rocks in the northern Yangtze Craton, suggest that slab subduction was the dominant geodynamic process responsible for Neoarchean magmatism and continental crust formation in the region. Moreover, on the basis of thermodynamic and trace element simulations, the estimated partial melting P–T conditions for the slab-derived TTGs were approximately 1.2 GPa and 830 °C. This corresponds to geothermal gradients of ~19–22 °C/km, suggesting a warm and intermittent subduction environment during the Neoarchean in the northern Yangtze Craton. Published: 2026-06-12T00:02:37+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.598 (consider)

However, the geodynamic processes of Neoarchean continental crust formation and evolution in the northern Yangtze Craton remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. (4) The ~2.5 Ga Douling dioritic gneisses and quartz dioritic gneisses exhibit characteristics typical of Archean sanukitoids and formed by partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and melts derived from sediments and oceanic slabs. These findings, together with the presence of contemporaneous subduction-related rocks in the northern Yangtze Craton, suggest that slab subduction was the dominant geodynamic process responsible for Neoarchean magmatism and continental crust formation in the region. Moreover, on the basis of thermodynamic and trace element simulations, the estimated partial melting P–T conditions for the slab-derived TTGs were approximately 1.2 GPa and 830 °C. item.title: Late Neoarchean garnet-biotite gneiss widely distributed in the Wulashan area, northwestern North China Craton: Implications for the late Neoarchean tectonic environment and the origin of ca. 2.45 Ga metamorphic zircons
item.dc:creator: Daomao Huang
Hangqiang Xie
Shuguang Li
Pengchuan Li
Shoujie Liu
Shan Ke
Chunyan Dong
Yusheng Wan
item.description: The late Neoarchean-early Paleoproterozoic garnet-biotite gneiss with high maturity has been identified in some areas of the North China Craton. Determining its exact formation age, distribution range and compositional features is crucial for understanding the process and timing of the preliminary cratonization during the Neoarchean. In this contribution, we report the field geology, SHRIMP UPb zircon dating, NdHf isotopic analysis, and whole-rock geochemical study of meta-sedimentary rocks (mainly garnet-biotite gneiss, named the Daqingshan Supracrustal Rocks) widely distributed in the Wulashan area, west of Daqingshan, northwestern North China Craton. Eleven samples dated reveal that detrital zircons and metamorphic zircons (including recrystallized zircons and overgrowth rims) show large age variations, mostly from 2.57 to 2.4 Ga and 2.5 to 1.9 Ga, respectively, with three samples having metamorphic zircon ages of 2.50–2.48 Ga. The presence of detrital zircons 2.4 Ga) mainly have εHf(t) values of 0 to +9, tDM1(Hf) of 2.8 to 2.5 Ga and tDM2(Hf) of 3.0 to 2.7 Ga. Therefore, the meta-sedimentary rocks of the Daqingshan Supracrustal Rocks are similar in geochemical and NdHf isotopic compositions and detrital zircon age distribution to the early-formed Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, supporting the inference that the detrital materials were mainly derived from a juvenile crustal source predominantly composed of the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, which would be much larger in distribution range than identified. Combined with previous studies in the Wulashan-Daqingshan area, which indicated that the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks were formed in an oceanic or oceanic island arc environment, the tectonic environment in the area has undergone significant changes from immature ocean or oceanic island arc to mature continental crust during the short period of the late Neoarchean (2.55–2.50 Ga). Published: 2026-06-10T23:56:47+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.596 (consider)

item.title: Neoproterozoic mafic complex in the Bikou microblock on the northwestern Yangtze Block: Implications for mantle source and tectonic evolution
item.dc:creator: Li Qin
Xianzhi Pei
Ruibao Li
Lei Pei
Chengjun Liu
Zuochen Li
Shaowei Zhao
Feng Gao
item.description: Neoproterozoic magmatic rocks exposed along the margins of the Yangtze Block provide key constraints for deciphering the tectonic evolution of the South China Plate and reconstructing its paleogeographic position within Rodinia. This study presents an integrated investigation of Neoproterozoic mafic complexes from the Bikou microblock in the northwestern Yangtze Block, using petrology, zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopes to elucidate their petrogenesis and tectonic implications. Results show that the Yanzibian mafic complex formed at 905–902 Ma, whereas the Cangshe mafic complex formed at 882–881 Ma. The Yanzibian mafic complex is characterized by enrichments in light rare earth elements (LREEs) and large ion lithophile elements (LILEs), with relative depletions in Nb, Ta, P, and Ti, exhibiting typical geochemical affnities of arc magmas. Zircon ε Hf(t) values range from +8.84 to +12.92, with t DM1 ages of 1097–931 Ma. High Nb/Zr ratios and relatively constant Rb/Y ratios suggest that these magmas were derived from the partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subduction-related melts. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Zircon ε Hf(t) values vary from +8.66 to +13.34, with t DM1 ages of 1086–905 Ma. Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia. Published: 2026-06-14T18:20:14+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.595 (consider)

The Yanzibian mafic complex is characterized by enrichments in light rare earth elements (LREEs) and large ion lithophile elements (LILEs), with relative depletions in Nb, Ta, P, and Ti, exhibiting typical geochemical affnities of arc magmas. Zircon ε Hf(t) values range from +8.84 to +12.92, with t DM1 ages of 1097–931 Ma. High Nb/Zr ratios and relatively constant Rb/Y ratios suggest that these magmas were derived from the partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subduction-related melts. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Zircon ε Hf(t) values vary from +8.66 to +13.34, with t DM1 ages of 1086–905 Ma. Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia.

item.title: Magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization in central Jiangnan orogenic belt, south China
item.dc:creator: Yong Zhang
Minjiang Tang
Qingfei Wang
Jinglei Xiao
Jiayong Pan
Fei Xia
Zhe Xu
Feipeng Dang
Guohua Wang
Shan Chu Han
Xia Zhang
Guang-Xian Liu
Junhao Chen
Huayang Jiang
item.description: The central Jiangnan orogenic belt in South China harbors a world-class magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization cluster, encompassing supergiant resources of tungsten (W), lithium (Li), copper (Cu), antimony (Sb), and gold (Au). This review synthesizes geological, geochemical, and geochronological data from key deposits to delineate two genetically distinct mineralization series: (1) A Cu-Au-W series associated with I- to S-type porphyry systems, characterized by potassic alteration and silicification; (2) An Nb-Ta-Li-W-Sn-Cu series linked to the evolution of S- to A-type granites, marked by sodic alteration, fluorine-rich muscovitization, and silicification. We propose a unified metallogenic model in which prolonged and episodic magmatism served dual roles: (i) as the source of granite-related rare metal and tungsten mineralization, and (ii) as the thermal engine driving regional hydrothermal circulation. This circulation leached Sb, U, and Au from Precambrian sedimentary sequences, re-precipitating them in structurally controlled sites as intermediate- to low-temperature vein-type deposits. The formation of these diverse yet genetically related deposits is interpreted as the result of extensive crustal magmatism within a unique extensional tectonic regime. This model provides a robust framework for future mineral exploration targeting in this metallogenic belt. Published: 2026-06-11T17:58:27+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.594 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

The central Jiangnan orogenic belt in South China harbors a world-class magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization cluster, encompassing supergiant resources of tungsten (W), lithium (Li), copper (Cu), antimony (Sb), and gold (Au). This review synthesizes geological, geochemical, and geochronological data from key deposits to delineate two genetically distinct mineralization series: (1) A Cu-Au-W series associated with I- to S-type porphyry systems, characterized by potassic alteration and silicification; (2) An Nb-Ta-Li-W-Sn-Cu series linked to the evolution of S- to A-type granites, marked by sodic alteration, fluorine-rich muscovitization, and silicification. We propose a unified metallogenic model in which prolonged and episodic magmatism served dual roles: (i) as the source of granite-related rare metal and tungsten mineralization, and (ii) as the thermal engine driving regional hydrothermal circulation. This circulation leached Sb, U, and Au from Precambrian sedimentary sequences, re-precipitating them in structurally controlled sites as intermediate- to low-temperature vein-type deposits. The formation of these diverse yet genetically related deposits is interpreted as the result of extensive crustal magmatism within a unique extensional tectonic regime. This model provides a robust framework for future mineral exploration targeting in this metallogenic belt.

item.title: Rare earth element enrichment through multi-stage magmatic differentiation and tectono-metamorphic processes: an example from the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, central Korean Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Vinod O. Samuel
Yirang Jang
Sanghoon Kwon
Sang-Tae Kim
Sungshil Kim
item.description: This study investigates igneous and metamorphic pathways of Rare Earth Elements (REE) enrichment in the Hongcheon carbonatite complex in the central part of the Korean Peninsula and its tectonic implications. This Fe- and P-rich carbonatite complex hosts an estimated 382,600 tons monazite ore reserve and is compositionally analogous to the world’s largest Mesoproterozoic Bayan Obo carbonatite complex in China. The Hongcheon carbonatite complex comprises four main units: a dolomite carbonatite (DC), a magnetite segment (MS), a phosphate segment (PS), and an aureole of Na-rich fenite. The DC unit shows an orthocumulate texture, composed of medium-grained dolomite with minor calcite, suggesting early stages of carbonatite melt differentiation. Whereas subsequent stages formed magnetite and phosphate-rich melt segments, the Na-rich fenite aureole indicates the expulsion of alkali-rich, especially Na-rich, melt/fluid from the carbonatite melt during crystallization. Our geochemical analyses indicate that interaction between the alkali-rich melt/fluid and the country rock does not result in significant REE concentration. Within the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, the PS hosts REE-rich minerals, such as apatite and monazite, whereas apatite and monazite are largely absent from the DC and MS units except where PS occurs. Textural relationships among minerals in the PS suggest that apatite might have preferentially crystallized in Ca- and P-rich melt. Extensive monazite mineralization occurred through apatite dissolution by fluid-assisted recrystallization under amphibolite facies metasomatic conditions, with subsequent calcite and quartz precipitation in the matrix and fracture-filling veins. These results and published (Permo-)Triassic monazite age data suggest that extensive monazite formation was likely influenced by tectono-metamorphic processes, reflecting later metamorphic overprinting shortly after peak (Permo-)Triassic collisional orogeny in the central Korean Peninsula. Overall, this study highlights that Ca-phosphate melts may have an important role in extracting REEs from carbonatite melt, whereas reactive fluids associated with later tectono-metamorphic events likely influence the redistribution and final mineralogical composition of REE ores (e.g., phosphate, fluoride, etc.) in large carbonatite complexes. Published: 2026-06-10T15:25:50+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.590 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vinod O. Samuel; Yirang Jang; Sanghoon Kwon; Sang-Tae Kim; Sungshil Kim

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107372

Relevance: 0.590 (consider)

This study investigates igneous and metamorphic pathways of Rare Earth Elements (REE) enrichment in the Hongcheon carbonatite complex in the central part of the Korean Peninsula and its tectonic implications. This Fe- and P-rich carbonatite complex hosts an estimated 382,600 tons monazite ore reserve and is compositionally analogous to the world’s largest Mesoproterozoic Bayan Obo carbonatite complex in China. The Hongcheon carbonatite complex comprises four main units: a dolomite carbonatite (DC), a magnetite segment (MS), a phosphate segment (PS), and an aureole of Na-rich fenite. The DC unit shows an orthocumulate texture, composed of medium-grained dolomite with minor calcite, suggesting early stages of carbonatite melt differentiation. Whereas subsequent stages formed magnetite and phosphate-rich melt segments, the Na-rich fenite aureole indicates the expulsion of alkali-rich, especially Na-rich, melt/fluid from the carbonatite melt during crystallization. Our geochemical analyses indicate that interaction between the alkali-rich melt/fluid and the country rock does not result in significant REE concentration. Within the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, the PS hosts REE-rich minerals, such as apatite and monazite, whereas apatite and monazite are largely absent from the DC and MS units except where PS occurs. Textural relationships among minerals in the PS suggest that apatite might have preferentially crystallized in Ca- and P-rich melt. Extensive monazite mineralization occurred through apatite dissolution by fluid-assisted recrystallization under amphibolite facies metasomatic conditions, with subsequent calcite and quartz precipitation in the matrix and fracture-filling veins. These results and published (Permo-)Triassic monazite age data suggest that extensive monazite formation was likely influenced by tectono-metamorphic processes, reflecting later metamorphic overprinting shortly after peak (Permo-)Triassic collisional orogeny in the central Korean Peninsula. Overall, this study highlights that Ca-phosphate melts may have an important role in extracting REEs from carbonatite melt, whereas reactive fluids associated with later tectono-metamorphic events likely influence the redistribution and final mineralogical composition of REE ores (e.g., phosphate, fluoride, etc.) in large carbonatite complexes.

item.title: Fluid exsolution in porphyry-skarn magmas constrained from volatile-bearing minerals in the Late Triassic Saishitang complex, East Kunlun Orogen, China
item.dc:creator: Liang Pan
Jinyang Zhang
Yuanming Pan
Qin Huang
Qilin Wang
Jiaxing Chai
Lanxing Zhang
Zhenbing She
Changqian Ma
Jianwei Li
item.description: Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised. Published: 2026-06-08T15:48:01+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.587 (consider)

Authors: Liang Pan; Jinyang Zhang; Yuanming Pan; Qin Huang; Qilin Wang; Jiaxing Chai; Lanxing Zhang; Zhenbing She; Changqian Ma; Jianwei Li

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107367

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised.

item.title: Crustal architecture, accretionary evolution, and mineralization in the southern Central Asian orogenic belt (Solonker-Alxa region, Inner Mongolia) from the Paleozoic to early Mesozoic
item.dc:creator: Qiang Gao
Mao-Qiang Yan
Robert Moritz
Jan Marten Huizenga
Hong-Tao Zhu
Zhao-Tong Liu
Kun-Feng Qiu
Jun-Hao Wei
Jun Deng
item.description: Understanding continental crustal architecture and its control on mineral systems is fundamental to the earth sciences. Here we integrate zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock Nd-Hf isotopes, lithogeochemical data, and regional metallogenic records from Cambrian to Triassic magmatic rocks across the Solonker-Alxa region of Inner Mongolia to define crustal domains and their metallogenic significance. Two contrasting isotopic provinces are identified: (1) an enriched domain [εNd(t) < −4, εHf(t) < 0] encompassing the North China Craton−Alxa Block and (2) a depleted domain [εNd(t) > −4, εHf(t) > 0] typifies the Central Asian orogenic belt (CAOB). These domains are separated by the Chifeng-Bayan Obo fault, the area north of Chaganhua, and the Quagan Qulu-Tebai fault. Crustal thickness patterns show a belt of thinned crust (<40 km) along the Mandula, Solonker, Ganqimandu, Yingba, and northern Enger Us fault zones, marking the boundary between the Mongolia collage and the Tarim-North China collage. Variations in crustal thickness suggest that the Alxa Block is a distinct Precambrian terrane, separate from the North China Craton. The protracted subduction-accretion and slab rollback of the Paleo-Asian Ocean have driven significant crustal growth in the Solonker-Alxa region, with juvenile crust contributions estimated at 37% in the southern CAOB. Crustal maturation and felsic differentiation resulted from polyphase melting events involving mafic lower crust, oceanic crust, and the middle to upper crust. A systematic correlation exists between isotopic domains, crustal architecture, and metallogenic distribution. Depleted domains with thin crust (<50 km) preferentially host porphyry Cu (± Au), epithermal Pb-Zn-Cu, and volcanic massive sulphide-type Cu deposits, whereas enriched isotopic domains and their peripheries contain porphyry Mo, skarn Cu-Fe, vein-type Au deposits, and magmatic Cu-Ni sulfide deposits, indicating genetic links to ancient crustal recycling and metasomatized lithospheric mantle. This synthesis provides a coherent tectono-metallogenic framework for the Solonker-Alxa segment of the southern CAOB and offers guidance for mineral exploration. Published: 2026-06-09T19:58:54+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.585 (consider)

Understanding continental crustal architecture and its control on mineral systems is fundamental to the earth sciences. Here we integrate zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock Nd-Hf isotopes, lithogeochemical data, and regional metallogenic records from Cambrian to Triassic magmatic rocks across the Solonker-Alxa region of Inner Mongolia to define crustal domains and their metallogenic significance. These domains are separated by the Chifeng-Bayan Obo fault, the area north of Chaganhua, and the Quagan Qulu-Tebai fault. Crustal thickness patterns show a belt of thinned crust (<40 km) along the Mandula, Solonker, Ganqimandu, Yingba, and northern Enger Us fault zones, marking the boundary between the Mongolia collage and the Tarim-North China collage. Variations in crustal thickness suggest that the Alxa Block is a distinct Precambrian terrane, separate from the North China Craton. The protracted subduction-accretion and slab rollback of the Paleo-Asian Ocean have driven significant crustal growth in the Solonker-Alxa region, with juvenile crust contributions estimated at 37% in the southern CAOB. Crustal maturation and felsic differentiation resulted from polyphase melting events involving mafic lower crust, oceanic crust, and the middle to upper crust. A systematic correlation exists between isotopic domains, crustal architecture, and metallogenic distribution. Depleted domains with thin crust (<50 km) preferentially host porphyry Cu (± Au), epithermal Pb-Zn-Cu, and volcanic massive sulphide-type Cu deposits, whereas enriched isotopic domains and their peripheries contain porphyry Mo, skarn Cu-Fe, vein-type Au deposits, and magmatic Cu-Ni sulfide deposits, indicating genetic links to ancient crustal recycling and metasomatized lithospheric mantle. This synthesis provides a coherent tectono-metallogenic framework for the Solonker-Alxa segment of the southern CAOB and offers guidance for mineral exploration.

item.title: Limited role of continental arc in crustal growth: Insights from zircon U-Pb ages and Lu-Hf isotopes of late Paleozoic granitoid plutons from the northern North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Jing Ran
Hao Wang
Jin-Hui Yang
Jin-Feng Sun
Jing-Yuan Chen
Yu-Sheng Zhu
item.description: Continental arcs have been proposed as major sites of continental crust generation because their calc-alkaline compositions resemble those of the bulk continental crust. However, the actual contribution of continental arc magmatism to crustal growth remains highly debated. Here, we present integrated zircon U-Pb ages and Lu-Hf isotopes from sixteen samples of late Paleozoic granitoid plutons from the northern margin of the North China Craton (NCC) to evaluate the role of continental arcs in crustal growth. The plutons yield crystallization ages of 315–272 Ma and contain abundant inherited zircons with ages of ~2.5 Ga and ~ 1.9–1.8 Ga, corresponding to major tectonothermal events in the NCC basement. Magmatic zircons show predominantly negative εHf(t) values ranging from −20.6 ± 0.3 to −6.0 ± 0.4 and two-stage Hf model ages of 1.6–2.8 Ga, indicating derivation from ancient crustal sources. Simple binary Hf isotope mixing calculations indicate that the melt requires a dominant crustal component, with juvenile mantle-derived material contributing no more than 37% for the least evolved pluton (the Daguangding pluton) and < 18% for the other plutons. Large inter-pluton but limited intra-pluton Hf isotopic variations (less than 5 ε units, mostly <3 ε units) are best explained by source heterogeneity within the NCC lower crust, involving variable proportions of Neoarchean and Paleoproterozoic crustal domains. Our results demonstrate that late Paleozoic continental arc magmatism represents large-scale reworking of Archean-Paleoproterozoic crust with minimal addition of juvenile mantle-derived material. Comparison of the Gangdese arc, the Andean arc, and the northern NCC shows that continental arcs built on ancient cratons contribute little to crustal growth, and that post-Archean continental crust formed mainly in oceanic arcs prior to arc–continent collision. Continental arc magmatism primarily reworks and homogenizes pre-existing continental crust rather than producing new crust. Published: 2026-06-11T06:25:19+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jing Ran; Hao Wang; Jin-Hui Yang; Jin-Feng Sun; Jing-Yuan Chen; Yu-Sheng Zhu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108663

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Continental arcs have been proposed as major sites of continental crust generation because their calc-alkaline compositions resemble those of the bulk continental crust. However, the actual contribution of continental arc magmatism to crustal growth remains highly debated. Here, we present integrated zircon U-Pb ages and Lu-Hf isotopes from sixteen samples of late Paleozoic granitoid plutons from the northern margin of the North China Craton (NCC) to evaluate the role of continental arcs in crustal growth. The plutons yield crystallization ages of 315–272 Ma and contain abundant inherited zircons with ages of ~2.5 Ga and ~ 1.9–1.8 Ga, corresponding to major tectonothermal events in the NCC basement. Magmatic zircons show predominantly negative εHf(t) values ranging from −20.6 ± 0.3 to −6.0 ± 0.4 and two-stage Hf model ages of 1.6–2.8 Ga, indicating derivation from ancient crustal sources. Simple binary Hf isotope mixing calculations indicate that the melt requires a dominant crustal component, with juvenile mantle-derived material contributing no more than 37% for the least evolved pluton (the Daguangding pluton) and < 18% for the other plutons. Large inter-pluton but limited intra-pluton Hf isotopic variations (less than 5 ε units, mostly <3 ε units) are best explained by source heterogeneity within the NCC lower crust, involving variable proportions of Neoarchean and Paleoproterozoic crustal domains. Our results demonstrate that late Paleozoic continental arc magmatism represents large-scale reworking of Archean-Paleoproterozoic crust with minimal addition of juvenile mantle-derived material. Comparison of the Gangdese arc, the Andean arc, and the northern NCC shows that continental arcs built on ancient cratons contribute little to crustal growth, and that post-Archean continental crust formed mainly in oceanic arcs prior to arc–continent collision. Continental arc magmatism primarily reworks and homogenizes pre-existing continental crust rather than producing new crust.

item.title: Zircon–apatite geochemistry and isotope constraints on VMS fertility in a late cretaceous back-arc basin, Eastern Sakarya Zone, Türkiye
item.dc:creator: Hadi Shafaii Moghadam
Wenjiao Xiao
Orhan Karsli
Faruk Aydin
Massimo Chiaradia
William L. Griffin
Simge Oğuz-Saka
item.description: Volcanogenic massive sulphide (VMS) deposits are common in submarine back-arc environments, yet mineralization is unevenly distributed even among volcanic successions formed within the same tectonomagmatic system. Here we investigate felsic volcanic sequences associated with the Murgul VMS district and stratigraphically equivalent barren successions from the Hatila Valley in the Eastern Sakarya Zone, northeastern Türkiye, to evaluate how magma evolution and crustal magma transfer varied between fertile and barren domains within a single Late Cretaceous back-arc basin. Mafic rocks from both domains display comparable juvenile Nd-Hf isotope compositions, with εNd(t) = +1.3 to +3.7 and εHf(t) = +6.8 to +9.8 for Hatila rocks, and εNd(t) = +3.1 to +4.0 and εHf(t) = +8.6 to +10.0 for the Murgul VMS district, together with subduction-modified trace-element signatures, including elevated Th/Yb and Th/Nb ratios. These data indicate broadly similar mantle sources and slab contributions. In contrast, felsic rocks associated with VMS mineralization show restricted whole-rock εNd(t)–εHf(t) variability, with εNd(t) = +3.0 to +3.5 and εHf(t) = +8.3 to +9.2, limited intra-sample zircon εHf(t) dispersion, and coherent zircon trace-element arrays involving U, Th/U, and Nb/Ta versus Hf. Barren felsic suites show broader isotopic and geochemical dispersion, including whole-rock εNd(t) = −9.4 to +0.4, εHf(t) = −11.1 to +5.9, and zircon εHf(t) = −9.2 to +10.2. Zircon oxybarometry indicates broadly overlapping low- to moderately oxidized conditions in barren and fertile suites, with mean ΔFMQ values of +0.79 ± 0.87 and + 0.31 ± 0.34, respectively, suggesting that oxygen fugacity alone did not distinguish fertile from barren systems. Apatite compositions and Nd isotopes broadly parallel the zircon record, with Murgul apatite yielding εNd(t) = +1.6 to +3.6 compared with −1.0 to +2.2 in Hatila apatite. We interpret the fertile domain to reflect coherent juvenile felsic magma evolution, limited open-system crustal modification, and vertically connected magma transfer within a back-arc basin supplied by compositionally similar mafic magmas. These results highlight isotopic focusing, defined here as restricted isotopic dispersion expressed by limited whole-rock Nd-Hf variability and limited intra-sample zircon εHf(t) variation, together with accessory-mineral-scale geochemical coherence, as indicators of magmatic conditions favorable for VMS development in submarine felsic volcanic successions. Published: 2026-06-12T23:45:15+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.575 (consider)

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Volcanogenic massive sulphide (VMS) deposits are common in submarine back-arc environments, yet mineralization is unevenly distributed even among volcanic successions formed within the same tectonomagmatic system. Barren felsic suites show broader isotopic and geochemical dispersion, including whole-rock εNd(t) = −9.4 to +0.4, εHf(t) = −11.1 to +5.9, and zircon εHf(t) = −9.2 to +10.2.
